title: Branch Naming Conventions
intro: Branch names might seem trivial, but on a team with dozens of branches, good naming makes the difference between order and chaos. Think of it like labeling folders in a filing cabinet -- clear labels help everyone find what they need instantly. Swipe right for GOOD branch names and left for BAD ones.

teach.title: Branch Names = Folder Labels
teach.body: Imagine a filing cabinet where every folder is labeled "stuff" or "misc." You'd never find anything. Now imagine folders labeled "2024-taxes," "car-insurance," "medical-receipts." Instantly useful. Branch names work the same way -- good names make a project manageable, bad names create chaos.

teach.title: The Naming Convention
teach.body:
Most teams use a **prefix/description** pattern:

- `feature/user-authentication` -- Adding something new
- `fix/login-crash` -- Fixing a bug
- `hotfix/security-patch` -- Urgent production fix

Use lowercase letters, hyphens instead of spaces, and keep it short but descriptive.

teach.title: Branch Names = Street Addresses
teach.body: Imagine if your home address was just "my house" instead of "742 Evergreen Terrace." No pizza delivery, no mail, nobody could find you. Branch names are addresses for your work. `feature/dark-mode` tells everyone exactly where to find the dark mode work, just like a proper address tells the delivery driver exactly where to go.

teach.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
teach.body:
- **No spaces or capitals** -- Some tools handle them poorly
- **No vague names** -- `my-branch` or `test123` tells nobody anything
- **No novels** -- Keep it under 30 characters when possible
- **No special characters** -- Stick to letters, numbers, hyphens, and forward slashes

Good naming conventions signal professionalism. When a hiring manager or client sees well-named branches in your GitHub repos, they see someone who writes production-quality code -- and that's the difference between getting hired or getting passed over.
Name branches with a prefix and short description using lowercase and hyphens -- like `feature/dark-mode` or `fix/login-crash`.

cards.explanation: This follows a clear convention: a category prefix (feature/) followed by a descriptive name using hyphens. Anyone on the team can tell at a glance that this branch adds a new feature related to user authentication.

Whose branch? What does it do? When a project has 50 branches, 'my-branch' tells nobody anything. Use a descriptive name that explains what the branch is for, like 'feature/dark-mode' or 'fix/login-crash'.
Great name! The 'fix/' prefix indicates it's a bug fix, and the description clearly explains what bug is being fixed. A teammate can review the pull request and immediately understand the context.

This looks like a throwaway name. Even for experiments, use something like 'experiment/new-api-approach'. Names like 'test123' pile up as mystery branches that nobody is willing to delete because nobody knows what they contain.

cards.content: Feature/Add New Dashboard With Updated Charts And User Preferences
cards.explanation: Branch names shouldn't have spaces or capital letters (some systems handle them poorly). Keep it short and use hyphens: 'feature/dashboard-redesign'. The pull request description is where long explanations go.
